title: Industrial relations: RBA staff knock back 9.5pc wage rise offer
full_text:
Reserve Bank staff have rejected a proposed 9.5 per cent pay rise, sending governor Michele Bullock back to the bargaining table as she urges wage restraint across the economy and grapples with mounting cost pressures at the central bank.
The RBA's staff rejected the proposed three-year enterprise bargaining agreement in a ballot that closed on Thursday, with 54 per cent of employees voting no - a margin of just over 100 votes - according to an RBA source speaking on the condition of anonymity.
Under the rejected deal, central bankers would have received a 9.5 per cent pay rise over three years - 3.5 per cent in the first year, 3 per cent in the second and 3 per cent in the third.
The vote increases pressure on the RBA to resume negotiations with the Finance Sector Union over a revised agreement covering junior and mid-level staff across its 2000-strong workforce. The union is demanding the RBA enshrine work-from-home rights in the enterprise bargaining agreement. It has also called for a 12.5 per cent pay increase over three years, and an inflation-protection mechanism that would have triggered additional pay rises if prices rose faster than wages.
FSU national secretary Julia Angrisano said RBA employees sent a resounding message that they would not accept an agreement that left their wages falling "dangerously behind" inflation or comparable employers.
"RBA staff provide an essential service to Australia's financial system, and unless management is prepared to value their work with adequate pay, the RBA will struggle to recruit and retain the highly skilled workforce they need," Angrisano said.
"We call on the RBA to resume negotiations in good faith and come to the table with a competitive pay offer, increase paid parental leave entitlements, and provide sensible work-from-home protections."
The RBA declined to comment.
A mid-level economist at the RBA earns between $90,700 and $113,600. The RBA's proposal for a 9.5 per cent pay rise would have provided an employee at the top of the band a $10,800 pay rise over three years.
The FSU argued the RBA's offer fell short of inflation, currently at 4 per cent, and was lower than recent deals achieved with other employers.
CareSuper employees recently agreed to a 10.5 per cent pay rise over three years or the consumer price index, whichever was higher, effectively giving workers an inflation top-up if prices rose faster than wages. Aware Super workers recently rejected a 10 per cent pay rise offer, while federal public servants are seeking a 15 per cent increase over three years in negotiations with the Albanese government.
The optics of the RBA offering a meaningfully larger pay rise would be challenging, given Bullock has repeatedly called for wage restraint across the economy to help bring inflation under control.
The central bank is also grappling with mounting cost pressures, driven by a blowout in its head office renovation project - now expected to cost $1.2 billion - and rapidly rising operating expenses.
Since 2022, headcount has increased 43 per cent to 2039 employees, with particularly strong growth in IT and corporate support roles, while annual operating costs have climbed 74 per cent to $750 million.
The RBA is not bound by public service pay agreements, but in recent years its wage offers have broadly mirrored those negotiated by the federal government.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Michael Read
heading: Banking and Economy
